You thought you were alone through every stormâ¦ but Waheguru was carrying you the whole time.

In Sikhi, nothing is ever random. Every delay, every heartbreak, every moment you felt unseen â it was all witnessed. The One who created you never once looked away. When you didnât have strength, He became your strength. When you didnât have answers, He became your direction. When people left, He stayed.

Sometimes we spend years trying to control outcomes, trying to fix everything ourselves, carrying burdens that were never ours to carry. But Gurbani teaches something deeper â surrender is not weakness, it is trust. Trust that Hukam (Divine Order) is wiser than our plans. Trust that what is written for you cannot miss you. Trust that what leaves you was never meant to stay.

One day youâll look back and realise: the doors that closed protected you, the pain that broke you reshaped you, and the prayers you thought were unheard were actually being answered in ways you couldnât yet understand.

So place it in His hands. Do your karam (effort), keep your heart pure, remember His Naam â and walk forward without fear.

Because the One who brought you this farâ¦ isnât about to leave you now.

Are you using affirmations to achieve your goals?! â¥ï¸ let me know your favorite one in the commentsâ¦.

What if the only thing standing between you and your next levelâ¦ is the way you speak to yourself?

There was a quote by Bruce Lee that completely changed my life:

âDonât speak negatively about yourself, even as a joke. Your body doesnât know the difference. Words are energy and cast spells.â

The moment I understood thatâ¦ everything shifted.

I realized that every âI canât,â every doubt, every small joke about not being good enough was programming my future. And if words can weaken you - they can also build you.

So I made a decision.

I started speaking life over myself.
I started declaring power.
I started affirming who I am becoming.

I tell myself every single day:

â¢ I am a winner.
â¢ God is always with me.
â¢ Today is my day.
â¢ I can do it alone.
â¢ I am the best.

Not from ego - but from belief.
Not from arrogance - but from alignment.

Because the world responds to the energy you embody.
And when you walk like youâre chosenâ¦
When you speak like youâre protectedâ¦
When you think like youâve already wonâ¦

You move differently.
You decide differently.
You show up differently.

High performers, wealthy minds, champions - they understand one thing: Your identity creates your results.

So if you want a new life, you need new words.
If you want bigger wins, you need bigger beliefs.

Stop cursing your future with doubt.
Start blessing it with conviction.

Speak like a winner.
Walk like a warrior.
Think like youâve already arrived.

Your words are building your destiny.

What are you declaring over your life today?

These soul-stirring lines from Guru Arjan Dev Ji are the ultimate antidote to the anxiety we carry about our future and survival. The Guru asks a simple question: Why does the mind consume itself with worry over the "hustle" and the struggle for sustenance?

To ground us in faith, the Gurbani points toward the creatures living deep within solid rocks and stones. These beings have no means to work, no path to follow, and no way to "earn" a living, yet the Creator has placed their nourishment right in front of them.

What we need to understand is, if the Divine can sustain a tiny life form trapped inside a stone, why do we doubt that we will be looked after? It is a reminder to shift from a state of panic to a state of trust. We are encouraged to do our work, but to release the ego that tells us we are the sole providers. 

This Shabad invites us to breathe, let go of the "what-ifs," and realize that the same Power that created the universe is already mindful of our every need.

This Shabad is about being grateful, in realising that the Divine has never intentionally or unintentionally brought any grief upon us. 

That's not Divine's innate nature. 

Somewhere, somehow, we broke some rule of nature, or are suffering because of the actions of others. 

All we can do whenever we have the awareness, is to be in awe if what has been provided to us - through the vastness of nature and multitude of solutions.
Finding the peace that the world can't give. In every breath, in every situationâSatnam Waheguru." ð¸

Many misunderstand Hukam as fateâ¦ as if life is already decided and our actions donât matter.

But Sikhi teaches something far deeper.

Hukam is the divine order flowing through existence - the rhythm in which life unfolds. We are not here to control every outcome, but we are fully responsible for how we think, act, and serve within it.

Living in Hukam is learning to give your best with honesty, stand for truth with courage, and accept results with humility. It is not passivity. It is strength without ego.

When we stop fighting reality, we finally find peace within it.

God isn't your delivery boy of happiness. He is the supreme balance. Good and evil, rise and fall. Everything is flowing from the same source.

Baba Deep Singh Ji was born in January 1682.

But history remembers him for the moment the impossible unfolded.

When news reached him that Harmandir Sahib had been attacked and desecrated, he did not hesitate.
He made a vow that he would reach the Guruâs house, or his body would fall on the way.

On the battlefield, Baba Deep Singh Ji was struck by a blow that severed his head.
By all human understanding, life had ended.
The body fell.
The breath stopped.

Yet Sikhi does not end where logic does.

By unwavering faith and complete obedience to Guru Gobind Singh Jiâs hukam, Baba Deep Singh Ji rose again.
He lifted his own head, held it in his left hand, and continued walking toward Harmandir Sahib.

He did not walk in silence.
He fought.
With his Khanda in his other hand, he struck the enemy while advancing forward, never turning back.

This was not the body returning to life.
This was the soul refusing to abandon its promise.
A Gursikh who has already offered his head to the Guru cannot be defeated by the blade.

Baba Deep Singh Ji did not rise to escape.
He rose to complete his vow.

This is not a story meant to amaze.
This is the standard Sikhi sets for us.
Save this. Share this. Let this history live.

Katha: Giani Pinderpal Singh Ji (@gianipinderpalsinghji)

#babadeepsinghji #shaheedi #sikh #ardaas #harmandirsahib

Our actions are often seen only on the surface, through the lens someone is carrying. From there, they are quickly labelled as good or bad and the story ends.

When higher consciousness is at work, situations are seen differently. They become invitations to look deeper, to understand where an action truly came from, and to recognise the wound or pain asking to be healed.

If that inner work is avoided, the same patterns will keep repeating. May we be supported to go deeper, to heal, rather than believing limiting narratives about ourselves that keep us trapped.

In late 2024, Anthropic introduced MCP (Model Context Protocol) because LLMs needed a cleaner, safer way to interact with the outside world. In just a year it already has over 97 million SDK downloads and 2 weeks ago Anthropic announced itâs donating MCP to the Linux Foundation. 

MCP is like giving models a shared language to access tools, trigger workflows, and pull data. The entire point of MCP is to make LLMâs more capable in a standardized way.

AI concepts you must learn, part 1 

AI is full of jargon that can feel intimidating, but once you break it down, the concepts become clear. Here are 7 more terms every beginner should know: 

Tokenization is how text gets split into smaller units so models can process it. Different models tokenize differently, which impacts accuracy and efficiency. 

MCP Protocol creates a standard way for AI models to connect with tools, APIs, and external apps, making them more useful in real-world workflows. 

Agentic Workflow describes how AI systems plan, decide, and act in sequence instead of just answering questions, powering the rise of autonomous agents. 

Vector Embeddings turn text or data into numerical representations that capture meaning. They allow AI to compare similarities and build features like semantic search. 

Attention Mechanisms let models focus on the most relevant parts of an input, a breakthrough that powers the transformer architecture behind modern LLMs. 

Quantization and Pruning are techniques to make large models smaller and faster without losing much accuracy, enabling them to run efficiently in production. 

Multi Agent Systems involve multiple AI agents with different roles collaborating on complex tasks, an emerging approach for building adaptive and powerful AI applications.

The Watcher House (Westfield, New Jersey, 2014)

In June 2014, Derek and Maria Broaddus bought what they thought was their dream home â a beautiful six-bedroom house in Westfield, NJ. Just days after closing the sale, a letter arrived in their mailbox. It was handwritten, addressed simply to âThe New Ownerâ, and signed by someone calling themselves âThe Watcher.â

The letter said:

âMy grandfather watched the house in the 1920s. My father watched in the 1960s. It is now my time.â

âDo you know the history of the house? Do you know what lies within the walls?â

The writer mentioned the coupleâs three children by name and asked if they had âfound out what room each of them will sleep in.â

More letters followed. They got increasingly hostile, warning:

âI am pleased to know your names now, and the names of the young blood you have brought me.â

âHave they found what is in the walls yet?â

The Broadduses never even moved in. Terrified, they tried to sell the house, but the story had made headlines, scaring off buyers. To this day, no one knows who âThe Watcherâ was. Police investigated neighbors, DNA from the envelopes, and even past owners â but nothing stuck.

The house still stands, and locals say the mystery watcher might still be out there.

Pankaj Tripathi in an interview with Filmygyan advised everyone to surrender oneself to god when things go wrong, when the road seems blurry â¤ï¸

Trusting the Timing of Your Journey.

Lucy Haleâs message becomes a reminder that life moves with a rhythm that isnât always visible to us, and the moment we stop forcing outcomes, something softer and more meaningful begins to unfold. When you trust the timing of your life, you stop gripping so tightly to how things should happen and instead allow space for things to happen as they are meant to. Lucy describes how releasing control brings a kind of relief, because you realise youâre still steering your ship, but you donât have to push against the current. When youâre aligned, doing your best, and showing up with intention, the next step becomes learning to let life meet you halfway. That means stepping into the things that scare you, becoming more visible, and letting go of the weight of other peopleâs opinions. Thereâs freedom in choosing to act without overthinking the reaction, freedom in trusting that the timing of your growth is perfect even when itâs unpredictable. DIARY ENTRY: Shukrana ð¤

As a kid, I never really understood why my dad would start crying at the Gurudwara sometimes. He was a happy man, so why the tears?

I understand now though.

I wanted to see the sunrise at the Darbar Sahib and walked over pretty early in the morning. I sat next to the water and awed at the beauty of one of Sikhismâs holiest sites. The moment I bowed my head, tears began flowing from my eyes.

Shukrana.

I was filled with so much gratitude and thanked God so much for everything Iâve been blessed with. I asked for guidance as I continue though my life. I prayed for a pure heart and clean intentions. But at one point, I stopped praying. 

I stopped praying not because I had nothing left to ask for. As a flawed human, I also desire more. I stopped praying, because I realized everything Iâve ever needed and more has always found me at the right time. 

With no more prayers, I just had tears left in my eyes to express my emotions. My body was filled with so much peace. I pulled the chunni on my head down lower and just let the tears continue flowing until my heart was satisfied.

Moments like this are so important to stay grounded and realize how trivial our matters are in this huge universe. They donât have to happen explicitly be at a place of worship. They can happen anywhere. Even writing this, I feel the same rawness.

I hope as I continue to grow, so does my heart, mind, and gratitude.

In life, we often go through unexpected turns â setbacks, losses, delays, disappointments. From our limited view, they can feel like mistakes or detours. Everything in life happens according to Hukam â not randomly, but with divine order and purpose.

So when we say, âWhen God writes your story...â, weâre acknowledging that Waheguru (God) is the ultimate author of our lives. And when Waheguru is the writer, even the parts we donât understand â the hard chapters â are not accidents. 

Theyâre intentional, meaningful, and necessary for our growth, for our learning, or for our spiritual elevation.
Even pain becomes a tool. Even delays carry wisdom. Even silence has a message.

So instead of fighting the plot twist, Sikhism teaches us to accept it with trust â not as defeat, but as part of a divine script far greater than what we can see.

Thatâs Chardi Kala â the resilient optimism that comes from trusting Hukam.

The real question that sometimes feels harsh is: How painful does it need to get before we choose a life of deeper connection, a life that feels truly rich?
In my own journey, every time I slip back into days of depression or anxiety, I notice the same pattern: at first, I sit with the pain, allowing it to be there. But when it deepens and becomes more intense, the only doorway left is to step into the eternal now...to rest in the Divine and remember that everything else is temporary.

 I got tired of making so many friends (except Vaheguru),
 hoping someone would end my suffering.
 But only upon meeting my Beloved Guru did my suffering end;
 through Shabad, I attained Union with Vaheguru.

Source: Guru Amardas Ji, Ang 37, Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji

Reflection:
Suffering is an inseparable part of life â no matter how happy people may seem on social media.
 But true relief doesnât come from worldly connections.
We search in so many places for peace â in people, possessions, or distractions â
 but only the Guru, through the Shabad, can truly lift our pain.
And the âà¨¦à©à¨à©â (dukh) spoken of in Gurbani isnât just physical pain.
 Itâs something deeper â the ache of being disconnected from Vaheguru.
When we reconnect with that Divine Light through the Shabad,
 we donât just escape pain â we transcend it.

All of you must have been to some kind of trek in your life at least once. I remember mine when I went to Hemkund Sahib in 2011. At the time, it wasnât as easy as it is now. 

So halfway through the trek from Gobind Ghat, I had to take a Pithu (the local guys who carry you on their back). Before that, I had trouble enjoying the views or indeed the journey itself. However, just as I got on the Pithu, everything changed. I could enjoy the views, I loved the sound of the winds. All in all, I could just embrace the pristinely vibe of the place. 

In life, when Waheguru holds your hand and assists you, your perspective of the things around you changes in a similar way. You tend to be more upbeat, you find everything to be nice and handsome, and generally speaking, youâre just relaxed. 

So when you do your Ardaas, ask for Him to hold your hand. If that happens, everything else just fixes itself. â¤ï¸

What do you think? According to me thereâs a huuge difference between roasting each other and dissing each other & I feel like not many people understand that. Parish and I love roasting each other.. whether itâs pulling each otherâs leg, cracking inside jokes or making fun of who takes longer to get ready (itâs him!ð). Itâs lighthearted, fun and it actually brings us closer because itâs never coming from a place of hurt or disrespect.

But we never cross that line into dissing each other. For us dissing means attacking character, bringing each other down or saying things that would hurt or humiliate the other person just to prove a point. Thatâs a big NO-GO in our relationship.

We made a silent pact long ago that no matter how much we joke, tease or roastâ¦ we will never make each other feel small in front of anyone (because weâre a team) or even when itâs just the two of us. And honestly thatâs  been one of the biggest relationship hacks thatâs kept us going strong after all these years.

Respect first, always. Even in the jokesâ¤ï¸

Suffering and joy are both part of Godâs command (Hukam). Acceptance leads to freedom from ego and pain. Reaching out to others who face similar struggles has helped guide me back to the right path. This is something very personal for me.
Although Iâm afraid of oversharing online and being judged, lâve realized that if I had known someone else had experienced something similar, it would have lessened my burden. If even one person reads this and feels seen or understood, it will be worth more than a hundred peopleâs judgment.
Being diagnosed with a lifelong illness destroyed my mental well-being. I was embarrassed to tell people what was wrong and believed no one would understand how I could feel this way or have these kinds of thoughts.
Shifting my perspective changed my life. At my lowest points, I had no faith at all. Even though I was practicing Sikhi, I was so angry at God for letting me suffer that I began to hate and resent everything and everyone. My personality changed drasticallyâ| became an extremely bitter person and lost many of the good qualities I once had.
At some point, I had drifted so far that months had passed since I last listened to any paath or Bani.Then, unexpectedly, Sukhmani Sahib started playing in a random queue of songs. I felt an immediate wave of longing and sadness-yet also peace. I took that moment as a sign, God trying to redirect me. I realized I had been looking in all the wrong places. What I needed was right in front of me. I began to find answers through Bani:

There are people who walk with you for a season, some who lift you briefly, and others who quietly fade when life grows heavy. But the soul has one true companion, an unfailing anchor, one whose presence does not waver with time nor circumstanceâ¦ that One is God. Not just a teacher of words, but a guiding light. A friend in the truest sense. One who sees your faults yet never withdraws love based on conditions. One who pulls you out of the places even you forgot you were trapped in.

The world may reward effort with tangible, yet temporary achievements. However, the Guru rewards and blesses you with grace, which isnât something which can be bought or sold, rather earned. 

When you stumble, He doesnât shame youâHe steadies you. When you fall short, He uplifts you. And the most miraculous thing is that He doesnât give back equallyâ¦ He gives more. Double. Fourfold. He returns your smallest steps toward truth with blessings far beyond what you imagined.

This relationship isnât transactionalâitâs transformational. When you trust, when you lean into that guidance, your life begins to shift. Not always outwardly, but inwardly. Peace begins to bloom. Clarity returns. The weight of doing it all alone starts to lift. Because youâre no longer walking the path by yourself.

When the world grows distant, when no one seems to understand, let your heart rest in this: the Guru walks with you. Knows you. Carries you. And what He gives in return for even your smallest sincerity is not just kindnessâitâs greatness. Not for your name, but for your soul.

When you strive to grow closer to God, your path becomes clearer and more purposeful. Building a deeper relationship with Him requires faith, prayer, and trust. Through these practices, you open your heart and mind to His guidance, allowing Him to work in your life in ways that align with His divine plan.

As you draw nearer to God, you begin to recognize the signs and opportunities He places before you. His wisdom leads you toward doors that are meant to open, while gently closing others that are not part of your journey. Trust in His timing, knowing that He sees the bigger picture and desires what is best for you.

By placing your faith in Him, you align yourself with a source of boundless love and strength. This connection empowers you to face challenges with courage and embrace opportunities with gratitude. In seeking Him, youâll find the direction and purpose you need to thrive.

@radhasoamijee 
#radhasoamijee
The more we trust His plan, the less we struggle.

Everything happens according to Waheguruâs divine plan. When we accept His will, we find peace in every situation, trusting that whatever unfolds is part of a greater purpose. Whether it is a moment of joy or difficulty, we learn to accept Waheguruâs will with grace, for He knows what is best for us.

When we surrender to Waheguruâs Hukam, the worries of the world fade away, and we find comfort in the belief that everything is in His hands. Waheguruâs plan for us is always more beautiful than anything we could have imagined for ourselves.

In this acceptance, we find true peace, strength, and contentment.

âGive up your head, but forsake not those whom you have undertaken to protect. 
Sacrifice your life, but relinquish not your faith.â â Guru Tegh Bahadur Sahib Ji.â

Guru Tegh Bahdur Sahib Ji was the ninth of ten Gurus who founded the Sikh religion and was the leader of Sikhs from 1665 until his beheading in 1675. A poet, a thinker, and a warrior, Guru Teg Bahadur Ji carried forward the light of sanctity and divinity of Guru Nanak Dev Ji and the subsequent Sikh Gurus. In May 1675, Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji was approached by Hindu Pandits from the Kashmir region, seeking the Guru's intercession against the forced conversions of Hindus to Islam by the Mughal rulers of India. For supporting the Hindu Pandits by resisting these forced conversions, and for himself refusing to convert to Islam, Guru Teg Bahadur Ji was publicly executed via beheading at the imperial capital of Delhi on the orders of Emperor Aurangzeb. Today, Gurdwara Sis Ganj Sahib and Gurdwara Rakab Ganj Sahib stand at the sites of the beheading and cremation of Guru Jiâs body. 

Thheekar forh dilees sir, Prabh pur keeaa payaan,
Teg Bahadur see kirya, karee na kinahoo aan.
Teg Bahaadur ke chalat, bhayo jagat ko sok,
Hai hai hai sabh jag bhayo, jai jai jai sur lok.

Casting off his bodily vesture on the head of Suzerain Of Delhi; Teg Bahadur departed to the Realm of God.
None who came into the world performed such glorious deeds as him.
On his departure, there was dismay in the world.
This world cried, "Alas, Alas". The Heavens rang with greetings of victory.
(Guru Gobind Singh Ji)

Do you know the very interesting story behind the Sikh Aarti? While most of you would know that it was composed by Guru Nanak Dev Ji, whatâs not common knowledge is how and when it was written. 

When Guru Nanak visited the historic Jagannath Puri, he observed how the priests in the temple were obsessed with the intricacies of the arrangements to be made for the Aarti in the temple rather than the Aarti itself. Their concern seemed more about âhowâ the Aarti had to be performed. Their concern wasnât âwhatâ the Aarti meant. 

This didnât sit well with Guru Nanak and upon stepping outside of the temple, he composed the Sikh Aarti as it is today. One of the great compositions! â¤ï¸

ð How Childhood Trauma Looks Like

Trauma isn't like a visible obvious wound, sometimes itâs a grown adult who doesn't speak much, have correct posture or who chases love from wrong people.

Past experiences donât just vanish. They settle in the nervous system. They shape how we see ourselves, how we love, how we react to stress, how we trust others.

Most people walk around thinking theyâve moved on. But it shows up in self-sabotage, perfectionism, people-pleasing, or emotional numbness.

If this hit home, itâs time to heal deeper.

Kauda Bachan Kar Ke Kise Da Hirda Nahi Dukhaunda.

Detailed Meaning in English:

This hukam by Guru Gobind Singh Ji emphasizes the importance of speaking with kindness, compassion, and sensitivity. Words hold immense power â they can heal or harm, uplift or break someone. This command encourages Sikhs to be mindful of their speech and avoid using language that may hurt others emotionally or mentally.

Key Insights:

Power of Words:

Words have the potential to leave lasting impacts. A bitter or harsh comment can deeply wound someoneâs heart, even if said unintentionally. Guru Sahib reminds us to use words that inspire love, unity, and positivity.

Emotional Sensitivity:

People are often dealing with personal struggles, insecurities, or pain that may not be visible. Harsh words can worsen their emotional burden. This hukam encourages us to develop empathy and consider how our words may affect others.

Promoting Kindness and Humility:

Speaking with love and respect aligns with core Sikh values such as humility (nimrata) and compassion (daya). By choosing gentle words, we reflect the qualities of a true Gursikh.

Conflict Resolution:

In moments of anger or disagreement, itâs easy to react harshly. This hukam teaches patience and encourages constructive communication, ensuring conflicts are resolved peacefully rather than through bitterness.

Spiritual Growth:

Gurbani teaches that a pure tongue reflects a pure heart. By avoiding bitter speech, we cultivate inner peace, self-control, and align ourselves with divine virtues.

Practical Application:

In Family Life: Avoid harsh scolding; instead, offer guidance with love.

In Friendships: Be mindful of jokes or comments that may unintentionally hurt someone.

In Professional Life: Practice calm, respectful communication, even under stress.

On Social Media: Promote positive language instead of negativity or trolling.

Conclusion:

This hukam reminds us that true strength lies in controlling our words and ensuring they reflect kindness, respect, and understanding. By practicing this, we not only uplift others but also nurture our own spiritual growth and inner peace. #spiritualgurbani #hukam41 #52hukams

Jujube (or daechu) is known for its calming and immune-supporting properties. We use it in braises, soups, and teas. This is the tea we drink everyday. Itâs so concentrated with jujube, you can feel its effects almost immediately, esp. the calming one. 

For a honey version of this tea that doesnât require an Instant Pot, preorder our cookbook UMMA at the link in our bio. Or, comment UMMA, and Iâll DM you the preorder link. â£ï¸

Ingredients:
250g dried Korean jujube, rinsed 
16 cups water, divided 
100g peeled, chopped ginger 

Instructions: 
1. Soak dried jujube in 8 cups water in Instant Pot chamber until soft, about 30 minutes to 1 hour. Dont discard the water.

2. Snip jujube into thin pieces and put it all back in the water. 

3. Pressure cook in for 20 min. 

4. Add ginger and remaining 8 cups water. 

5. Click sautÃ©, cover until it boils. Once boiling, continue to boil using sautÃ© function for 30 min, covered.

6. Discard ginger and place softened jujube in strainer that fits over a large bowl. 

7. Press on jujube with back of a wooden spoon, pouring some of the jujube tea (in the instant pot) over it to extract jujube pulp. Repeat until youâre left with just jujube skin. Press on jujube skin once more and discard. 

8. Combine all strained tea together and mix. Serve warm and refrigerate leftovers in an airtight container. Be sure to mix jujube tea before serving.
